mission.title: Install and Configure Operator Framework on Kubernetes
mission.description: The Operator SDK is a framework for building Kubernetes applications, known as Operators, which simplifies the development process by providing high-level APIs and project scaffolding. Installing the Operator SDK allows you to create and manage Kubernetes-native applications more effectively.

steps.title: Add the Operator SDK Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Operator SDK Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add operator-sdk https://operator-framework.github.io/operator-sdk-helm-charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install the Operator SDK
steps.description:
Install the Operator SDK using Helm with the following command:
```bash
helm install operator-sdk operator-sdk/operator-sdk --namespace operator-sdk --create-namespace --version v1.42.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Operator SDK pod is running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n operator-sdk
```

steps.title: Configure RBAC for the Operator SDK
steps.description:
Create a Role and RoleBinding to allow the Operator SDK to manage resources in the cluster:
```bash
kubectl create role operator-sdk-role --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete --resource=pods --namespace=operator-sdk
kubectl create rolebinding operator-sdk-rolebinding --role=operator-sdk-role --user=system:serviceaccount:operator-sdk:default --namespace=operator-sdk
```

steps.title: Set resource limits for the Operator SDK
steps.description:
Update the Operator SDK deployment to set resource limits:
```bash
kubectl patch deployment operator-sdk -n operator-sdk --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/resources", "value": {"limits": {"cpu": "500m", "memory": "512Mi"}, "requests": {"cpu": "250m", "memory": "256Mi"}}}]'
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of the Operator SDK will show the Operator SDK pod running in the 'operator-sdk' namespace, and the RBAC configurations will allow it to manage Kubernetes resources. You can also verify that resource limits are applied correctly.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add operator-sdk https://operator-framework.github.io/operator-sdk-helm-charts
helm repo update
helm install operator-sdk operator-sdk/operator-sdk --namespace operator-sdk --create-namespace --version v1.42.0

kubectl create role operator-sdk-role --verb=get,list,watch,create,update,patch,delete --resource=pods --namespace=operator-sdk
kubectl create rolebinding operator-sdk-rolebinding --role=operator-sdk-role --user=system:serviceaccount:operator-sdk:default --namespace=operator-sdk

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Operator SDK release using Helm to remove the deployment from the cluster. Run the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall operator-sdk --namespace operator-sdk
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were created by the Operator SDK. Execute the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd <crd-name>
# Replace <crd-name> with the actual CRD names associated with the Operator SDK.
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the namespace used for the Operator SDK and verify that it has been removed. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace operator-sdk
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources. You can export the current deployment and any relevant CRDs with:
```bash
kubectl get deployment operator-sdk -n operator-sdk -o yaml > operator-sdk-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade the Operator SDK to the latest version. Run the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade operator-sdk operator-sdk/operator-sdk --namespace operator-sdk --version v1.43.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check if the Operator SDK pod is running the new version successfully after the upgrade:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n operator-sdk
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Operator SDK pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors. Use the following command:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n operator-sdk
# Replace <pod-name> with the name of the pod in question.
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If the Operator SDK cannot access certain resources, verify the Role and RoleBinding. Check the current roles with:
```bash
kubectl get role -n operator-sdk
kubectl get rolebinding -n operator-sdk
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the pod is being killed due to out-of-memory (OOM) issues, consider increasing the memory limits. Check the current resource usage with:
```bash
kubectl top pod -n operator-sdk
```

troubleshooting.title: Helm upgrade fails with version conflict
troubleshooting.description:
If the Helm upgrade fails due to version conflicts, ensure that the specified version exists in the repository. Check available versions with:
```bash
helm search repo operator-sdk/operator-sdk --versions
```

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running and both Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
